item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Protect delicate baby skin
item.description: Huggies Essential Nappies stand as a reliable choice for parents, offering a strong absorbency, high comfort and thoughtful design that makes care for your child easier. You don’t have to worry about quality when deciding to buy Huggies. The nappies feature a soft and stretchy waistband that ensures a snug fit without restricting movement.
item.content:encoded:
Huggies Essential Nappies stand as a reliable choice for parents, offering a strong absorbency, high comfort and thoughtful design that makes care for your child easier. You don’t have to worry about quality when deciding to buy Huggies.

The nappies feature a soft and stretchy waistband that ensures a snug fit without restricting movement.

This is particularly important during the early days of your baby’s life when they are constantly wriggling and exploring.

The dual leg protection helps prevent leaks, ensuring a peace of mind whether your child is moving during the day or asleep.

Huggies Essentials are designed with a three-way blowout protection and up to 12 hours of absorbency, keeping your baby dry and comfortable for longer.

This makes it very useful for overnight use as it requires less changes and uninterrupted rest for both you and your baby.

Parents can feel reassured knowing that their baby’s nappies are made with no added nasties – free from formaldehyde, elemental chlorine, and natural rubber latex. This commitment to safety reflects Huggies dedication to protecting delicate baby skin while maintaining high performance standards.

At birth, healthy infants have delicate skin which can be up to 30% thinner than adult skin, making them more susceptible to irritation.

Infants born prematurely have an even more fragile skin at birth, which increases their risk of skin damage and irritation.

While your baby’s skin will develop throughout the first year of life, it is important that gentle care and protection are provided to ensure your baby’s skin stays healthy. Huggies have been caring for babies’ skin for over 30 years.

Safety isn’t just a priority, it’s a non-negotiable standard built into every product Huggies make.

A product of Kimberly Clark, Huggies Essential Nappies is distributed in Fiji by Motibhai Group – sharing quality, sharing happiness.

Far left: Ana shops for Huggies Essentials at Extra supermarket, Laucala Beach.

Inset: This commitment to safety reflects Huggies dedication to protecting delicate baby skin while maintaining high performance standards.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Benefits of fruits to you
item.description: ACCORDING to health.com, fruits and vegetables are an essential part of a healthy diet, yet many people unknowingly throw away one of the most nutritious parts the skin. The outer layer of many fruits and vegetables contains valuable nutrients, including fiber, vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants that help support overall health.
item.content:encoded:
ACCORDING to health.com, fruits and vegetables are an essential part of a healthy diet, yet many people unknowingly throw away one of the most nutritious parts the skin.

The outer layer of many fruits and vegetables contains valuable nutrients, including fiber, vitamins, minerals, and antioxidants that help support overall health.

By eating produce with the peel on, you can gain more nutritional benefits while also reducing food waste.

One of the biggest advantages of eating fruit and vegetable skins is the extra fiber they provide.

Fiber plays an important role in digestion by helping food move smoothly through the digestive system.

It can also help people feel full for longer periods, which may support healthy weight management.

In addition, fiber helps maintain stable blood sugar levels and supports heart health by lowering cholesterol.

However, many people worry about dirt, bacteria, or pesticide residue on produce skins.

Fortunately, fruits and vegetables can be cleaned safely and easily.

Before eating or cutting produce, rinse it thoroughly under running water.

Firm produce such as potatoes, carrots, or apples can be gently scrubbed with a clean produce brush.

It is also important to wash produce even if you plan to peel it because bacteria from the outer surface can transfer to the inside during cutting.

Avoid using soap or bleach, as plain water is usually enough. Bruised or damaged areas should also be removed before eating.

Apples are one of the best examples of a fruit that should be eaten with the skin on. Apple skins contain a large amount of the fruit’s fiber, and removing the peel can significantly reduce its nutritional value.

The skin also contains antioxidants such as quercetin, which help protect the body’s cells from damage caused by harmful molecules known as free radicals.

These antioxidants may reduce inflammation and support gut health by limiting the growth of harmful bacteria in the digestive system.

Peaches are another nutritious fruit whose skin offers many health benefits. Peach skin contains fiber, vitamins A and C, and powerful antioxidants.

One important compound found in peach skin is chlorogenic acid, which has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties.

Studies suggest that chlorogenic acid may help lower blood pressure and cholesterol levels, supporting better heart health.

Eating fruits and vegetables with their skins is a simple habit that can improve nutrition and overall well-being.

While it is important to clean produce properly, there is no need to discard these nutrient-rich outer layers.

By enjoying apples, peaches, and many other fruits and vegetables with the peel intact, people can make healthier choices and get the most from the foods they eat.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | A grandmother’s love never fades
item.description: At 62, Maria Marama thought her days of raising children were long behind her. But after tragedy struck her family, the grandmother from Lalaji in Navosa stepped back into the role of caregiver once again, this time for her grandchildren. What was once meant to be a simple shopping trip quickly became another reminder of the responsibilities she now carries. With quiet strength and determination,
item.content:encoded:
At 62, Maria Marama thought her days of raising children were long behind her. But after tragedy struck her family, the grandmother from Lalaji in Navosa stepped back into the role of caregiver once again, this time for her grandchildren.

What was once meant to be a simple shopping trip quickly became another reminder of the responsibilities she now carries. With quiet strength and determination, Maria continues to support her family following the passing of her daughter-in-law, proving that a mother’s love does not fade with age.

Maria now spends close to $600 on groceries each time she shops, carefully budgeting to ensure there is enough food and essentials for the household. Recently, she even had to make an extra trip to buy snacks and milk for the children.

“I usually spend around $600 on shopping and once our rations finish then we come back to shop,” she said.

Despite the increasing cost of living, Maria says her family is fortunate to have access to fresh produce from their farm. They grow root crops such as cassava and dalo, while their yaqona plantation remains the family’s primary source of income.

“We have a huge yaqona plantation and that is our main source of income for my family,” she shared.

Maria lives with her son and grandchildren, but only a few weeks ago the family was shaken by heartbreak. Her daughter-in-law, who had quietly battled a terminal illness without seeking early medical assistance, passed away after spending just two days in hospital.

The sudden loss left the family devastated, particularly her son, who was struggling to cope with the death of his wife.

Maria said she encouraged him to stay strong and not allow grief to consume him, reassuring him that she would help care for the children during the difficult period ahead.

“With what happened, I told my son not to stress too much because I will help look after the grandchildren,” she said.

Now, at an age when many begin slowing down and enjoying retirement, Maria has once again become the nurturing figure her family desperately needs. From preparing meals to managing shopping expenses and caring for the children, her days are once again filled with the responsibilities of motherhood.

Although life has become more challenging, Maria remains practical when it comes to stretching the family budget. She says she carefully compares prices before making purchases and prefers shopping at RB Patel in Sigatoka because of its affordability and wide range of products.

“For me, I choose RB Patel to shop in because they have the best prices in Sigatoka,” she said.

Her advice to families facing financial pressure is simple, take the time to compare prices and shop wisely.

“When out shopping, spend some time going through the supermarket and comparing prices. Then only you will know where it is best to shop.”

Through grief and hardship, Maria continues to stand tall for her family. Her story is one of sacrifice, resilience and unconditional love, a powerful reminder that even in life’s most painful moments, family remains at the centre of everything.

Maria prefers to shop at RB Patel Sigatoka, because of their affordable prices.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Eggs prove their place in a healthy diet
item.description: Eggs have long been considered one of the most affordable and nutritious foods available, with research continuing to highlight their many health benefits when consumed in moderation. Packed with essential nutrients, eggs can support muscle growth, brain function, bone strength and even weight management. One large egg contains around 6.3 grams of protein, including all nine essential amino acids
item.content:encoded:
Eggs have long been considered one of the most affordable and nutritious foods available, with research continuing to highlight their many health benefits when consumed in moderation. Packed with essential nutrients, eggs can support muscle growth, brain function, bone strength and even weight management.

One large egg contains around 6.3 grams of protein, including all nine essential amino acids required by the body. Protein plays a vital role in maintaining muscles, bones and skin, making eggs a valuable addition to daily meals. Nutrition experts suggest adults should consume between 1.2 and 1.6 grams of protein per kilogram of body weight, meaning eggs can contribute significantly towards meeting those needs.

Eggs may also help with weight control. With only 72 calories per egg, they provide a filling source of nutrition without excessive calorie intake.

Studies have shown that people who eat eggs for breakfast often feel fuller for longer and may consume fewer calories later in the day.

Their low glycaemic index also helps avoid sudden spikes in blood sugar levels, which can benefit people living with type 2 diabetes.

Another major benefit lies in bone health.

Eggs contain vitamin D, which is essential for maintaining strong bones and reducing the risk of osteoporosis. Research suggests that eating eggs regularly may help improve vitamin D levels, particularly during winter months when sunlight exposure is reduced.

Despite containing dietary cholesterol, modern research indicates that moderate egg consumption has less impact on blood cholesterol than previously believed.

For most healthy individuals, eating up to seven eggs per week is considered safe and may even improve certain blood fat levels when combined with regular exercise.

Eggs also support eye and brain health. Rich in vitamin A and choline, they contribute to healthy vision, memory and nervous system function.

Some studies have even linked moderate egg consumption with a reduced risk of dementia later in life.

While debate over eggs and long-term health continues, experts agree that moderate consumption as part of a balanced diet can offer a wide range of nutritional benefits.

Eating eggs helps maintain strong bones in our body.

Eggs have long been considered one of the most affordable and nutritious foods available. Pictures: freepik.com

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | VERE’S TAKE | There is shrinkflation and skimflation
item.description: For many households the weekly supermarket trip is becoming a stressful exercise. You head to the checkout with a trolley containing the same items you bought a year ago, only to discover the bill is significantly higher every visit. Figures may suggest inflation is easing, but for many households the reality feels more costly. The cost of feeding a family and buying everyday essentials continues
item.content:encoded:
For many households the weekly supermarket trip is becoming a stressful exercise. You head to the checkout with a trolley containing the same items you bought a year ago, only to discover the bill is significantly higher every visit.

Figures may suggest inflation is easing, but for many households the reality feels more costly. The cost of feeding a family and buying everyday essentials continues to climb.

This growing gap between economic indicators and lived experience is one reason why calls for a formal inquiry into supermarket pricing practices are gaining momentum. The Consumer Council of Fiji has raised concerns about transparency in the retail sector. Chief executive, Seema Shandil, has renewed calls for a market inquiry, arguing that consumers deserve a clearer understanding of how prices are determined and why grocery bills continue to rise.

Consumer advocates have pointed to cases where shelf prices differ from those charged at the checkout, particularly on promotional items.

While a few cents here and there may seem insignificant, those discrepancies add up over time and can have an impact on families already struggling with tight budgets. There are also growing questions about supermarket specials and promotions delivering the savings they promise.

Discounts and bundle deals are designed to attract shoppers, but consumers are increasingly asking whether the advertised bargains represent value for money. A colleague said his family paid $40 less when they calculated their shopping manually.

Hidden inflation often goes unnoticed. Viliame says his fortnightly grocery bill has increased dramatically over the past several months.

Where he once spent $150 on household groceries, he now spends more than $200 for the same items. His shopping list includes basics such as butter, salt, potatoes, onions, peanut butter, mayonnaise, cooking oil, canned fish, meat, cocoa, milk, dhal, biscuits and beetroot.

Even with home-grown vegetables such as rourou, bele, spinach and dhania helping to offset costs, he says supermarket spending continues to rise.

Viliame has noticed changes in product sizes and quality. He questions whether meat portions are getting smaller and says he prefers village chicken, despite paying $40 to $50, because processed ones shrink further when defrosted.

The rising cost of eggs is another concern. For many families, eggs have long been considered an affordable source of protein.

Today, however, consumers are increasingly questioning prices that can reach up to $19 a tray. Adding to this is the issue of shrinkflation, a practice that has attracted growing attention recently.

Opposition MP Premila Kumar warned that shrinkflation is quietly increasing the cost of living by reducing the quantity of products while maintaining the same price. Several common household items have undergone noticeable reductions in size recently.

Powdered milk, reportedly dropped from 450 grams to 400 grams. Toilet paper packs fell from 1200 sheets to 800 sheets, while washing soap was reduced from 900 grams to 800 grams. For consumers, the effect is simple.

They are paying the same amount, or more, for less. Kumar argues that consumers should be informed whenever product sizes are reduced. Her position raises an important question: should authorities focus only on prices, or should they also monitor value?

After all, the cost of living is measured by how much purchasing power consumers receive for their hard-earned dollars. Leader of NFP Biman Prasad acknowledged concerns about shrinkflation (decreased size) and skimflation (reduced quality), saying the Price Monitoring Enforcement Task Force is examining such practices to improve transparency. At the same time, he points out that Fiji cannot escape global economic realities. Higher shipping costs, fuel prices and import expenses continue to affect the cost of goods.

Fiji remains vulnerable to international market pressures. Recognising these challenges does not diminish the need for greater transparency. Consumers understand that businesses face rising costs.

But shoppers still deserve accountability. If prices must rise, explain why.

If product sizes are reduced, disclose it clearly. If promotions are advertised, ensure the savings are genuine.

At a time when people are carefully counting every dollar, consumers deserve honesty that the price they see reflects the value they receive.

Be vigilant shoppers, check your goods against your receipts!

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Maria supports her family
item.description: At 62 years old, Maria Marama from Lalaji in Navosa has once again taken on the responsibility of raising children after the tragic passing of her daughter-in-law. Now caring for her grandchildren, Maria continues to support her family with strength and determination despite the emotional and financial challenges they face.
item.content:encoded:
At 62 years old, Maria Marama from Lalaji in Navosa has once again taken on the responsibility of raising children after the tragic passing of her daughter-in-law.

Now caring for her grandchildren, Maria continues to support her family with strength and determination despite the emotional and financial challenges they face.

Maria spends close to $600 on groceries during each shopping trip, carefully budgeting to provide food and essentials for the household. Alongside her son and grandchildren, the family relies on income from their yaqona plantation while also growing root crops such as cassava and dalo on their farm to help reduce food expenses.

The sudden loss of her daughter-in-law deeply affected the family, especially her son, but Maria reassured him that she would help care for the children during this difficult period.

Despite her age, she has stepped back into the role of caregiver, managing household duties and supporting her family through grief.

Maria also encourages families to compare supermarket prices carefully and shop wisely to manage the rising cost of living.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Upgrade your game day experience
item.description: As the excitement builds for the Valladolid (Spain) Sevens this weekend and the countdown begins to the FIFA World Cup, there’s never been a better time to upgrade your home entertainment setup with Courts. Whether you’re cheering for your favourite rugby sevens side or preparing for football’s biggest stage, Courts is helping customers bring the stadium atmosphere home with its exciting TV Trade-
item.content:encoded:
As the excitement builds for the Valladolid (Spain) Sevens this weekend and the countdown begins to the FIFA World Cup, there’s never been a better time to upgrade your home entertainment setup with Courts. Whether you’re cheering for your favourite rugby sevens side or preparing for football’s biggest stage, Courts is helping customers bring the stadium atmosphere home with its exciting TV Trade-In Promotion.

Customers can now trade in their old TV and upgrade to a brand-new television from any leading brand available at Courts such as Tesla, Hisense, Toshiba and LG.

From affordable smart entertainment to premium cinematic viewing, there’s a perfect option for every household and budget.

Bigger screens, sharper picture quality, smarter features, and immersive sound mean every tackle, try, goal, and replay feels larger than life.

But why stop at just a TV? This sporting season, Courts is encouraging customers to create the ultimate match-day entertainment zone.

Pair your new TV with a powerful Mini Hi-Fi System brand like LG for stadium-quality sound that puts you right in the middle of the action.

Add a compact bar fridge with brands like Haier and Hisense to keep your drinks cold and within arm’s reach, and a Hisense and LG microwave for quick snacks during halftime or extra time, so you never have to miss a single moment of the game.

Whether it’s family rugby nights, football watch parties with friends, or simply enjoying the convenience of modern smart living, Courts Fiji has everything needed to transform your lounge into the ultimate sports hub.

Every purchase is backed by Courts Fiji’s trusted after-sales support and extended warranty options, giving customers added peace of mind long after the final whistle.

This season don’t just watch the game. Experience it bigger, louder, and better with Courts.

This is also the final week of the Extended Courts Mother’s Day and Hisense promotion.

So, hurry! You still have three more days to take advantage of the amazing combo deals and FOC deals while they end.

Also, if you spend $100 or more on any Hisense product you go into the draw to win a Hisense Refrigerator.

Scan the QR code for quick updates on OB and red pen deal locations and the latest red pen deals through our social media channels!

Courts has also launched it’s myCourts app.

The app is built to simplify the customer experience by bringing shopping, account access, and customer support together in one easy-to-use platform.

Customers can also explore current promotions, featured products, and exclusive offers directly from their mobile device. Scan the QR code to download the myCourts app.

Add a compact bar fridge with brands like Haier to keep your drinks cold and within arms reach.

Spend $100 or more on any Hisense product and you go into the draw to win a Hisense refrigerator.

Upgrade your home entertainment setup with Courts. Picture: MELI NAGUSUDRAU

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: TIMES SHOPPER | Bringing every moment to life
item.description: Entertainment at home feels different when the picture is bigger, brighter, and more immersive. The new arrival Aiwa QLED TV range at Narhari is designed to make those moments feel bigger, brighter, sharper, and more immersive than ever before. Built with advanced QLED technology, smart entertainment features, and sleek modern styling, the new Aiwa range transforms everyday viewing into a premium
item.content:encoded:
Entertainment at home feels different when the picture is bigger, brighter, and more immersive. The new arrival Aiwa QLED TV range at Narhari is designed to make those moments feel bigger, brighter, sharper, and more immersive than ever before. Built with advanced QLED technology, smart entertainment features, and sleek modern styling, the new Aiwa range transforms everyday viewing into a premium home entertainment experience.

The Aiwa 55″ QLED TV is perfect for modern homes looking for stylish entertainment with powerful performance. Compact yet immersive, this model delivers vibrant Quantum Dot colours, crisp 4K Ultra HD clarity, and smooth streaming that instantly upgrades movie nights and everyday viewing. Whether it is Netflix, YouTube, Disney+, or live sports, every scene feels rich and detailed with realistic colour and enhanced brightness. Its slim edge-to-edge bezel design adds a modern premium touch that fits beautifully into bedrooms, apartments, and family living spaces.

For homes that love bigger entertainment, the Aiwa 65″ QLED TV delivers a more cinematic viewing experience with enhanced colour depth and impressive screen presence. Equipped with webOS Hub smart technology, Dolby Audio, MEMC motion enhancement, and voice command features, this model is designed for smooth and effortless entertainment.

Fast action scenes feel clearer. Sports feel more exciting. Music videos feel more vibrant. Whether it is weekend football, family movie nights, or streaming the latest series, the 65″ model creates an immersive atmosphere that brings everyone together.

The Aiwa 75″ QLED TV is designed for those who want entertainment on a grand scale. Massive, bold, and incredibly immersive, this television turns ordinary viewing into a true cinema experience at home.

The giant display combined with Quantum Dot colour technology delivers stunning brightness, deeper contrast, and lifelike visuals that pull you into every scene. From blockbuster action movies to gaming nights and live sports events, every detail feels larger than life. Its premium design and edge-to-edge display also make it a standout centrepiece for modern Fiji homes.

The new Aiwa QLED TV range combines smart convenience, premium visuals, immersive sound, and modern design into one complete entertainment solution for today’s families. More colour. More clarity. More reasons to gather together.

📍 Visit your nearest Narhari branch today in:

Suva, Lautoka, Nadi, Ba, Yadua-Sigatoka, Rakiraki, Nausori, Navua, Savusavu, Nakasi and Labasa.

Visit our website: https://www.narharifiji.com/

Follow us for the latest deals, promos & tech updates:

: https://www.tiktok.com/@narhari_officialaccount

Narhari – Quality solution for your every room.

Discover the quantum Leap in colour clarity and cinematic entertainment.

Step into a smarter world of entertainment with AIWA webOS Hub TV.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
